> we could actually cheat and enable an exhale-specific internal setting
I tend to think that if the app code can be structured to help testing, there should not be any specific testing code in the app itself. What I've done with the @no_run decorator below is to monkeypatch ``deploy.explode()`` so that it does nothing when required. This way there is no need to clutter the code with some testing or debugging if/else statements!

It's generally better to break down the code in functions or methods that can be tested or disabled individually than adding 'break points' in a huge function.

Actually, the return value can be an arbitrary object:
- `unicode` if the field is a string.
- `datetime` if the field is a timestamp.
- Or `bool`, or `NoneType`, or `int`.
- Can also be a JSON `dict` or `list`.
- In the future, when we implement #140, it will be a `BaseAPIJSONObject`.

So I would either document this as returning `object`, or just not document anything. The reason I say you can get away with not documenting it, is because most magic methods have well-defined signatures. In the case of `__getitem__` for a generic `Mapping`, we know it takes a `Hashable`, and returns some `object`. Since we're not returning a more specific type here, we don't necessarily need to document it.

When the query is pickle to the session the operators *and*, *or* and *not* are prefixed with `_`, so: `_AND`, `_OR` and `_NOT`

```python
In [1]: q = db(db.auth_user.id > 0) & (db.auth_user.id < 10)

In [2]: q
Out[2]: <Query (("auth_user"."id" < 10) AND <Set ("auth_user"."id" > 0)>)>

In [3]: q.as_dict()
Out[3]: 
{'_db': <DAL uri="sqlite://storage.sqlite">,
 'db': <DAL uri="sqlite://storage.sqlite">,
 'first': <Query ("auth_user"."id" < 10)>,
 'ignore_common_filters': False,
 'op': <bound method SQLiteDialect._and of <pydal.dialects.sqlite.SQLiteDialect object at 0x7f897554ff90>>,
 'optional_args': {},
 'second': <Set ("auth_user"."id" > 0)>}

In [4]: q.as_dict(flat=True)
Out[4]: 
{'first': {'first': {'fieldname': 'id', 'tablename': 'auth_user'},
  'ignore_common_filters': False,
  'op': 'lt',
  'optional_args': {},
  'second': 10},
 'ignore_common_filters': False,
 'op': '_and',
 'optional_args': {}}
```

The difference between extras and env markers is that installing extras is a **user**'s choice (like feature flags, and extra names may even be treated as a kind of public API), on the other hand environmental markers force requirements to be installed under certain conditions pre-defined by **developer**, it's not user's choice.

Environment markers constraints are useful in case you need some extra library to be installed in certain env type. i.e. win32 may require some shims or Python 3.3 100% requires installing asyncio from PYPI, since it's not shipped as a part of its stdlib.

I'd say leave it under extras w/o env markers, but just move to `setup.py`.

This is the block that made me think this change was worth making.  If something inside the block fails, obj._cross_validation_lock will be stuck on `True`.  

I think this happens to be harmless here, because we're assuming that we're being called from `obj.__new__`, which means an uncaught exception will prevent a live instance to the invalid object from ever existing, but repeating this pattern elsewhere would result in hard-to-find bugs where the relative ordering of validators would cause very different behaviors.

Here's what PEP-8 has to say about the leading underscore:

> _single_leading_underscore: weak "internal use" indicator. E.g. from M import * does not import objects whose name starts with an underscore.

In this case, `_retrieve_params` should be considered a property of `StripeObject`. Because `ListObject` (a descendant of `StripeObject`) already accesses it, we could consider its visibility to be currently "protected" (based on the definition from other OO languages). `ListableAPIResource` is also a descendant of `StripeObject` so we're not really modifying that visibility from what it was before at all.

The idea of protected visibility is also in-line with PEP-8's idea of internal use, so I think we're okay on this here.

> Regarding fuel aggregation by Nova Scotia, would you mind sending me the json file? 

The [`currentmix.json`](https://github.com/r24mille/pyiso/blob/bc2e61e527bc06180ac2e78368e9fc01c918366c/tests/fixtures/nsp/currentmix.json) file I added to the unit test fixtures gives a fully example. I'll copy the element for one timestamp here for discussion:

```
"datetime":  "\/Date(1507114800000)\/",
"Solid Fuel":  41.99,
"HFO/Natural Gas":  13.08,
"CT\u0027s":  0.07,
"LM 6000\u0027s":  9.59,
"Biomass":  0.98,
"Hydro":  2.74,
"Wind":  30.64,
"Imports": 0.92
```

The section of code that this comment and my other comment in this pull request is the mapping of Nova Scotia Power's fule names to pyiso fuel names. Here it is in its entirety:

```
fuels = {
    'Solid Fuel': 'coal',
    'HFO/Natural Gas': 'natgas',
    'CT\'s': 'oil',
    'LM 6000\'s': 'thermal',
    'Biomass': 'biomass',
    'Hydro': 'hydro',
    'Wind': 'wind',
    'Imports': 'other'
}
```

My justifications are:
* Solid Fuel : `coal`- The term "Solid Fuel" is used to describe both coal and petcoke. Though the percentages described in their [Thermal Electricity](https://www.nspower.ca/en/home/about-us/how-we-make-electricity/thermal-electricity/default.aspx) page suggest this value is primarily their coal facilities.
* HFO/Natural Gas : `natgas`- This stands for Heavy Fuel Oil/Natural Gas
* CT's : `oil`- I think that CTs are diesel (oil) combustion turbines. I based this interpretation on their [Oil Facilities](http://www.nspower.ca/en/home/about-us/how-we-make-electricity/thermal-electricity/oil-facilities.aspx) page which describes the three combustion turbines in their service region.
* LM 6000's : `thermal` - As discussed above, my interpretation is based on [the Tuft's Cove 6 power proposal document I found](http://www.novascotia.ca/nse/ea/tuftscove6/NSPI-TuftsCove6-Registration.pdf).
* Biomass : `biomass`
* Hydro : `hydro`
* Wind : `wind`
* Imports : `other` - This relates to my [other comment](https://github.com/WattTime/pyiso/pull/161#discussion_r143331357) on this pull request.

So, given your feedback, that CHP is currently maps to `natgas` in the pyiso fuel types. I propose one of two solutions:
1. We add `chp` as a base fuel type for pyiso and the `NSPowerClient` uses it.
2. In the parser, add both the "HFO/Natural Gas" value and "LM 6000's" value together in a single row for the `get_generation(...)` results since they both map to the same pyiso fuel value.

I prefer option 1 above, but I don't feel strongly about that.

On any unrelated note:

> Fossil, as you might have noticed, is used in NYISO, and it actually represents coal.

Should we open an issue to fix this in NYISO? Change mappings for NYISO's `fossil` value to `coal`?

I think you need some sort of sleep or message block or something in here... because in the degenerate case where `len(submitting_tasks) >= self._parent.threads` (so you've got your requests in flight), but you call `ExtendedTaskOperations._handle_output` and no request is actually _done_ yet, which means the `finished_task_queue` is empty, which means `_handle_output` exits immediately, which means you go back to your while loop conditional (which is still true!) - you're in a tight loop there.

This is where `Future` completions are really nice because you don't have to poll (at least not directly), but you can't use that since you're in 2.7.

I think really the problem you have is that if you don't block here you'll end up creating more `threading.Thread` objects and issuing more requests, which you don't want to do because you want to honor the requested degrees of parallelism. It might be better since we don't have `Future`s to just create a pool of `N` workers upfront, and have the worker method pulling from the queue, and exit when there's nothing left... at a high level something like this:

1. Populate queue with all pending tasks
2. Create N worker threads
3. Each worker thread pulls 100 messages from the queue, and invokes the "handler" on that 100.
4. The handler does your logic (issue request, if failure due to max message size then div by 2 and try again in the same worker thread recursively like you do now).
5. Worker thread adds tasks which are done to the done queue.
6. Repeat from step 3 in the same worker thread until there's nothing left in the queue at which point the thread method gracefully exits

Meanwhile you can call `thread.Join()` on each worker and thus avoid a specific timeout.

Alternatively you can keep your current implementation and do the same thing but with a blocking queue read -- you'll have to be careful that you know there will be more data coming into the queue (or else you'll be stuck blocking forever).

The direction we are moving is to keep nbextension/jupyterlab extensions well separated from the server extensions. A couple of reasons for this:

* A single server could be serving notebooks to multiple frontends (notebook, nteract, jupyterlab)
* We are in the process of separating the notebook server into its own project and repo that won't share any code with the frontends
* We are also moving to a different approach for installing all of these things that will make it all easier for users. While we do this, we want to avoid making things more complicated with our existing stuff.

Obviously, in nbgrader, we could wrap all that and expose an endpoint that does it all automatically. For that I think the best approach is to create a conda-forge feedstock with post install scripts that do everything. This is what we are doing with the vega package and the install process is then trivial (no post install commands needed). Are you ok with that approach? -If the action is PASSED, the status of step will be updated as PASSED.

You can have one application be related to another multiple times.  Let's say you have an application that needs two different databases for some reason.  It would have two different named relations in its `metadata.yaml` that both use the `postgres` interface protocol.  You would have to call `add-relation` twice.  On the postgresql side, it would just have the one `db` relation and that relation would have two different relation IDs that happen to point to the same remote application.

From the reactive point of view, in the postgresql interface layer, the instance would have two items in `self.applications`, one for each relation ID.  Each of those application instances would have the same application name, and the same number of units, but those unit instances would be distinct and could have different data (requesting different database names).  Data that you set on the postgresql's side of each of those relations would be independent, though all units of the remote application would see the same data (again, postgresql is saying "this is my data for this relation ID"; in reactive, the `application` instances actually map to a relation ID and maybe that means we should call them `relations` instead of `applications` but I thought that might be more confusing).

It should be fine to pass any function to `map_fn`. The function given to `map_fn` takes in tensors and returns a tensor; unlike a `py_func`, it doesn't have access to the actual data stored in the tensors, just their nodes. It's only invoked during graph construction time, not at session runtime.

In other words, `py_func` is more like a Python _kernel_, while `map_fn`'s callback just defines a graph transformation that uses existing kernels.

Googling around, I found [this](https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/faq/coreutils-faq.html#The-ls-command-is-not-listing-files-in-a-normal-order_0021) which explains that sorting order is based on your locale. While I think sorting here is only a nicety and not essential (if order really matters, you can wrap the commands in a script in the order you want them run), it'd be kind of nice if we had this behavior.

In seeing how we can do this in Python, I saw the first bullet point [here](https://docs.python.org/3/howto/sorting.html#odd-and-ends). Unfortunately, at least testing this on a couple Ubuntu machines, neither approach described there works for me when using the locale as configured by Ubuntu/Debian tooling on a couple different machines. (The `locale` shell command works, but the methods in the locale module do not. `locale.getlocale()` returns `(None, None)`.)

If you think this a peculiarity with my systems and this would work in almost all other places (or you know of another approach), I'd be happy to make this change. Barring that though, I think we should just stick with the current sorting order and I'll update the documentation to clarify this minor point.
